utterances: Sarah, do you...
Well, there's also been a decline in abortion...
Yeah.
...which I think is an important thing to underscore here.
The underlying pregnancy rate is going down.
Teen abortion is going down.
Teen births are going down.
And I think that sort of the solid direction we're all heading is the big picture here.
What these court cases are about, and this particular discussion is about, is sort of at the margin of what is really a good story, which is less pregnancy amongst teen-agers.
Laura, thank you very much for the call.
Thank you.

When we come back from a short break, we'll continue our discussion on how families handle teen pregnancy and abortion, and an update from France, where voters may decide the fate of the EU Constitution this weekend.
That's after the break.
I'm Neal Conan.
You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
(Announcements)
This is TALK OF THE NATION.
I'm Neal Conan in Washington.
And here are the headlines from some of the stories NPR News is following today.

The Senate has confirmed Judge Priscilla Owen to the Federal Appeals Court, four years after she was first nominated by President Bush, and the Senate has begun debate on the nomination of John Bolton to be the next US ambassador to the United Nations.
That nomination is also controversial; several senators have expressed concern over Bolton's temperament.
You can hear more on those stories later today on ""All Things Considered"" from NPR News.
Tomorrow on TALK OF THE NATION, sunset provisions in the Patriot Act could actually mean a new dawning of FBI powers if things go their way on Capitol Hill.
We'll look at the future of the controversial law tomorrow on TALK OF THE NATION.
Today we're talking about parental notification laws, how parents and teens deal with unplanned pregnancies.
We want to hear your stories.
If you're a medical professional who's had to enforce the notification law, a teen or a parent who's struggled with this decision, our number is (800) 989-8255; (800) 989-TALK.
The e-mail address is totn@npr.org.
Our guests are Sarah Brown, director of the National Campaign to Prevent Teen Pregnancy, and Kay Hymowitz, a contributing editor to City Journal and a senior fellow at The Manhattan Institute.
And let's get another caller on the line.
This is Susan.
Susan's with us from La Jolla in California.
Hello.
Hi.

I had an abortion when I was 15 years old, and I was having unprotected sex at the time, and part of the reason for that was because my parents are extremely conservative Republicans and my mother chose not to ever speak to me about sex or protected sex.
I did choose to tell my mother, because I was terrified and didn't even know how to go about having an abortion, and...
And how did that conversation go?
Rami Khouri and Hirsh Goodman joins us now.
Rami Khouri is editor-at-large for the Lebanese newspaper The Daily Star.
He joins us from the BBC studios in Beirut.
Hirsh Goodman is a political columnist for the Jerusalem Report.
He joins us from NPR's bureau in Jerusalem.
And gentlemen, thanks very much for being with us.
Thank you.
It was incredibly wonderful.

My mother, despite the fact that she was, you know, appalled that I had been having sex, appalled that I had been having unprotected sex, able to find a very deep compassion within her to realize that for me to, first of all, be pregnant, and secondly, to even try to have a baby, would have been a very traumatizing experience.
You know, my parents have six kids, and for them, pregnancy and childbirth is a beautiful thing.
But they were able to understand that, for me, it was a quite different situation.

And I follow abortion politics and conversations and issues very avidly, and I'm really concerned that--I very rarely hear the word `compassion' when people speak about abortion, especially with young women, because I think that is almost more important than the idea of women's rights or even legal issues.
Mm-hmm.
I feel that many people who are pro-life don't have compassion and don't try to understand that pregnancy and childbirth is not right for everybody.
I wonder, Susan, just getting back to your story for a moment--you were 15 years old.
Did you and your parents get the boy involved in the conversation and his parents?
No.
Did you tell your parents who the father was?

I did, and actually, funnily enough, the boy--the young man--did come down to meet with me when I had the abortion, and my father, who I was literally afraid would, you know, kill me, as you said earlier, you know, came into my room at one point and said, you know, `I'm really upset with this young person and, you know, I'm upset with you, but I love you, and so, you know, I'll allow him to be with you while you have this abortion.'
Hm.
And, you know, my parents are both health-care practitioners, and maybe that's why they were able to find this compassion.
I'm not sure.
But, you know, I was extremely lucky, and many, many women are not as lucky as I am, and they often get overlooked.

There's this idea that, you know, women can just go and talk to somebody and decide that abortion's not right for them and have a baby and everything will be right and perfect, and that's just not the way it goes.
It's not reality.
Susan, thank you so much for the phone call.
OK.
Bye-bye.
We appreciate it.
Sarah and Kay, I wanted to ask you--I did ask her about the boy's involvement here because, clearly, they have to play a role, too.
Kay Hymowitz?
Well, one would hope so.

But, you know, I think inevitably, when you have a young person--a 15-year-old, as in this case--who is pregnant, the relationship with the boy may or may not be a big part of what she's going through.
And what I mean by that is just that kids have sex for all kinds of reasons and in all kinds of relationships.
Ideally, sure, if a child has gotten pregnant, it's because she's in love and very close to a young man, but it's not always the case.
And I'm not sure how much families want to involve the young man.
It seems to me this is a very personal question and depends on the situation.
Sarah?
Well, I think this raises the issue of parents taking the time to actually find out what has happened.
A lot of people will just make assumptions that maybe it's the steady boyfriend or whatever.
They will jump to conclusions.
In fact, if you talk with young women who've had these pregnancies, there are many, many stories.

There are stories of older men who've gotten them pregnant, the boyfriend in the house of the mother; there are stories of date rape, of girls passing out at parties and not knowing what happened to them.
Sometimes there's love, true.
But it's a very sensitive and critically important thing to know, in fact, what did happen to this young girl.
And I do think that there's a lot more interest now in involving the young man.
For example, if this young woman chooses to carry this baby to term, we now have much stricter enforcement of child support and paternity.
So, you know, we have to say to this young man, you know, `You will be a father.
You will be responsible for this child, and it doesn't matter if you move to another state or enlist in the military.
And that is largely something that's developed more strongly in the last few years.
So, you know, the notion that this is just one person now, I think, is beginning to erode.
I think people are beginning to understand that there's more involved here than just the girl.
Let's talk now with Lucy.
Lucy's calling from Allentown, Pennsylvania.
Yes.
Hi.
Hi.
Yes.
I've worked extensively as a volunteer in this area.
I accompanied girls to court as a volunteer for seven years, hundreds of girls, and spent a good bit of time with each.
And this is in a state, presumably, where they're going to court so that they don't have to notify their parents?
Yeah.
It's to get a judicial bypass.
Right.
And I just won't say where it was.
We've moved from the area; that's the only reason I stopped doing it.
Uh-huh.
But it was set up extremely well there.
The court process was very simple.
The judges were sympathetic.
There--we didn't use the judges that weren't; we had the option.
All the people that dealt with these girls were very sympathetic.
The whole--it was very clear that the only issue the judge was supposed to consider was the maturity of the girl.
But I would talk to them a lot so that they would, you know, really have--be very clear, in case the judge asked them any questions.
Of course, they'd already been through counseling, but these girls, you know, really can use all the counseling they can get.
Thank you.
